agent_output: {"executive_summary":"A logistics API experiencing a gradual P99 latency increase from 200ms to 1800ms over two weeks, with no deployment correlation and normal database metrics, points strongly toward external API degradation — most likely one of the three external dependencies (geocoding, carrier rates, or payment gateway) is experiencing progressive slowdown. The gradual nature rules out code regressions and suggests either upstream service degradation, connection pool exhaustion, or resource leak. The fact that requests succeed but slowly, with no errors, is the classic signature of a dependency with no timeout or with an overly generous timeout.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Logistics API","responsibility":"Orchestrates shipment operations by coordinating geocoding, carrier rate lookups, and payment processing","boundaries":"Inbound HTTP requests, outbound calls to three external APIs and database"},{"name":"Geocoding Service (External)","responsibility":"Resolves addresses to coordinates","boundaries":"External dependency, likely HTTP REST"},{"name":"Carrier Rates API (External)","responsibility":"Provides shipping rate quotes from carriers","boundaries":"External dependency, likely HTTP REST"},{"name":"Payment Gateway (External)","responsibility":"Processes payment transactions","boundaries":"External dependency, likely HTTP REST"},{"name":"Database","responsibility":"Persistent storage for logistics data","boundaries":"Internal dependency, query times reported as normal"}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Geocoding Service","contract":"HTTP request with address, response with coordinates","coupling_type":"temporal — request blocks on response"},{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Carrier Rates API","contract":"HTTP request with shipment details, response with rate quotes","coupling_type":"temporal — request blocks on response"},{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Payment Gateway","contract":"HTTP request with payment details, response with confirmation","coupling_type":"temporal — request blocks on response"},{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Database","contract":"SQL queries","coupling_type":"temporal — confirmed normal latency"}],"data_flow":"Inbound request → database lookups (normal) → external API calls (geocoding, rates, payment) → response assembly → outbound response. The critical path includes all three external calls, and if they are sequential, total latency is additive.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Sequential External Call Chain","rationale":"Likely current pattern — each external call waits for the previous one, making total latency the sum of all three"},{"pattern":"Missing Circuit Breaker / Timeout","rationale":"No errors reported despite 9x latency increase means the system waits indefinitely (or with very generous timeouts) for slow responses rather than failing fast"}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Shipment Orchestration","classification":"core","aggregates":["Shipment","Route","Quote"]},{"name":"Payment Processing","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Payment","Transaction"]},{"name":"Address Resolution","classification":"generic","aggregates":[]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Geocoding Service","downstream":"Logistics API","relationship":"Conformist — API accepts whatever the geocoding service provides"},{"upstream":"Carrier Rates API","downstream":"Logistics API","relationship":"Conformist — API conforms to carrier data model"},{"upstream":"Payment Gateway","downstream":"Logistics API","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — payment gateway likely has SLAs"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Investigate external API latency before internal causes","gains":"Highest probability root cause given the evidence pattern (gradual degradation, no errors, normal DB)","sacrifices":"Could delay finding an internal cause if one exists","alternatives_considered":["Investigate memory/GC pressure first","Investigate connection pool exhaustion first","Investigate thread starvation first"]},{"decision":"Recommend adding per-dependency latency instrumentation","gains":"Definitive diagnosis within hours, permanent observability improvement","sacrifices":"Requires code change to add tracing","alternatives_considered":["Analyze access logs with response time bucketing","Use network packet capture","Add APM tool"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"External API call strategy","evolution_path":"Move from sequential to parallel calls where data dependencies allow — geocoding and payment are likely independent of carrier rates lookup"},{"area":"Timeout and circuit breaker configuration","evolution_path":"Add configurable timeouts per dependency, then add circuit breakers with fallback behavior"},{"area":"Caching layer for geocoding","evolution_path":"Address-to-coordinate mappings are highly cacheable — add a cache to eliminate repeated geocoding calls entirely"}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"External API contracts","reason":"Cannot control response times or behavior of third-party services — must design defensively around them"},{"area":"Payment gateway call ordering","reason":"Payment must occur after rate confirmation — this sequential dependency cannot be parallelized away"}],"risks":[{"risk":"Hypothesis 1 (MOST LIKELY): One or more external APIs experiencing gradual degradation. The carrier rates API is the prime suspect — rate APIs often slow down as carrier catalogs grow or as rate-limiting kicks in with increased usage. The gradual nature matches a service slowly degrading under its own load or throttling your requests progressively.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Add per-dependency latency logging immediately. Contact external API providers to check their SLA dashboards. Check if your API key tier has rate limits that degrade gracefully (slow responses instead of errors)."},{"risk":"Hypothesis 2 (LIKELY): Connection pool exhaustion or leak. If HTTP client connections to external APIs are not being properly released, the pool gradually fills. New requests wait for a connection to become available. This perfectly explains gradual degradation with no errors — connections eventually free up, just slowly.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Check HTTP client connection pool metrics. Look for connections in CLOSE_WAIT state. Verify that response bodies are being fully consumed and connections released. Run `netstat` or equivalent to check connection states to external API hosts."},{"risk":"Hypothesis 3 (MODERATE): Memory leak causing increasing GC pressure. A slow memory leak would cause progressively longer GC pauses, adding latency to all requests. The gradual nature fits, and GC pauses would not show up in database query dashboards.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Check heap usage trends over the two-week period. Look for sawtooth patterns with rising baseline. Check GC logs for increasing pause times. A simple restart would temporarily fix the symptom if this is the cause — if latency drops after restart and gradually climbs again, this is confirmed."},{"risk":"Hypothesis 4 (MODERATE): DNS resolution degradation. If DNS TTLs are being ignored and lookups happen per-request to external APIs, a DNS resolver issue would add latency to every external call. This is subtle and often missed.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Check if HTTP clients cache DNS lookups. Monitor DNS resolution times. Check /etc/resolv.conf and DNS server health."},{"risk":"Hypothesis 5 (LOWER): Sequential external API calls amplifying small per-call degradation. If all three APIs each added 500ms, total request time increases by 1500ms — matching the observed delta almost exactly (200ms → 1800ms = +1600ms, roughly 530ms per API).","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Even after fixing the root cause, parallelize independent external calls to reduce baseline latency and limit blast radius of future degradations."},{"risk":"Hypothesis 6 (LOWER): SSL/TLS session cache expiry or certificate chain verification issues. If TLS sessions are not being reused, full handshakes on every request add significant latency, especially to three different external endpoints.","severity":"low","mitigation":"Verify TLS session reuse is enabled in HTTP clients. Check for certificate chain changes at external API endpoints."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Immediate triage in this order: (1) Add per-dependency latency instrumentation — wrap each external API call with timing and log it. This will identify which dependency is slow within minutes. (2) Check connection pool state — run `netstat -an | grep ESTABLISHED` and `netstat -an | grep CLOSE_WAIT` against external API IPs. (3) Check heap/memory trends — if your runtime has GC, check for rising memory baseline. (4) Quick validation test — restart the service and observe if latency immediately drops. If it does, you have a leak (connection or memory). If it doesn't, the problem is external. (5) Long-term — add timeouts (e.g., 2s per external call), circuit breakers, parallel calls where possible, and caching for geocoding.","files_to_create_or_modify":["Add middleware or wrapper for external API calls with per-call latency logging","Add health check endpoint that probes each external dependency independently","Add timeout configuration (recommend 2000ms per external call, 5000ms total request timeout)","Add circuit breaker configuration per external dependency"],"patterns_to_follow":"Bulkhead pattern (isolate external dependencies), Circuit Breaker pattern (fail fast on degraded dependencies), Timeout pattern (never wait indefinitely), Cache-Aside pattern (cache geocoding results). Use structured logging with dependency name, latency, and request ID for correlation.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do not add a single global timeout — use per-dependency timeouts so you can identify which service is slow","Do not retry slow requests without backoff — this amplifies load on an already struggling dependency","Do not ignore the root cause just because adding timeouts masks the symptom","Do not assume database dashboards tell the whole story — they measure query execution time, not connection acquisition time or result serialization"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":78,"high_confidence_areas":["The root cause is NOT the database — dashboard confirms normal query times","The root cause involves external API calls or resource management around them","The gradual nature eliminates deployment-related code regression","Adding per-dependency latency instrumentation will definitively identify the cause"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which specific external API is degrading (need instrumentation to confirm)","Whether the issue is on the external side vs connection management on the client side","Whether there is one root cause or multiple contributing factors"],"assumptions":["Database dashboard accurately reflects actual query performance including connection pool wait times","The three external API calls are made sequentially (if parallel, the math changes)","No infrastructure changes (DNS, network, load balancer) occurred during the two-week period","The 10,000 requests/day volume has been relatively stable"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"A logistics API experiencing a gradual P99 latency increase from 200ms to 1800ms over two weeks, with no deployment correlation and normal database metrics, points strongly toward external API degradation — most likely one of the three external dependencies (geocoding, carrier rates, or payment gateway) is experiencing progressive slowdown. The gradual nature rules out code regressions and suggests either upstream service degradation, connection pool exhaustion, or resource
